summary: Introduced named fighter animation ID enums, replacing many raw numeric animation/subaction IDs in common and character MotionState tables. The PR added ftCo_AnimId for common actions, character-specific ft*_AnimId enums in forward.h headers, and updated ftData_MotionStateList plus per-character ft*_Init_MotionStateTable entries to use symbolic names. The author noted the community often calls these IDs "subactions", that the exact naming was flexible, and that many names were inferred from motion names with some unknown/missing entries left as UnkNNN.
what_changed: Added a large ftCo_AnimId enum to src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/forward.h, beginning with ftCo_AnimId_None = -1 and including common animation IDs such as ftCo_AnimId_DamageFall, ftCo_AnimId_Attack11, ftCo_AnimId_AppealR, and ftCo_AnimId_Count.
Added character-specific ft*_AnimId enums across many fighter forward.h files, generally starting at ftCo_AnimId_Count so character animation IDs continue after the shared common range.

Replaced raw integer animation IDs in src/melee/ft/ftdata3.c's ftData_MotionStateList with named ftCo_AnimId constants, e.g. -1 became ftCo_AnimId_None, 29 became ftCo_AnimId_DamageFall, and 0 became ftCo_AnimId_DeadUpFallHitCamera.

Replaced raw integer first fields in per-character MotionState tables with character or common animation ID symbols, e.g. Captain Falcon entries changed from 295, 296, etc. to ftCa_AnimId_SwordSwing4, ftCa_AnimId_BatSwing4, and later special-move IDs.

Used shared/common animation IDs where appropriate in character tables, e.g. Young Link/Link-related tables used ftCo_AnimId_AppealR and ftCo_AnimId_AppealL for appeal entries while using ftLk_AnimId_* for Link-specific actions.
Preserved unknown or weakly identified entries as UnkNNN-style enum names rather than inventing precise action names.
Touched 55 files with 2450 added and 1366 deleted lines, mostly header enum additions plus one-for-one replacement of numeric IDs in initialization/data tables.
smart_moves: Converted opaque numeric table fields into searchable symbols without changing the surrounding MotionState table structure or callback wiring.
Anchored character-specific animation ID enums at ftCo_AnimId_Count, making the common-vs-character ID split explicit and reducing hardcoded offset mistakes.
Inferred names from existing motion names, which made the new constants align with known MotionState and callback naming while avoiding unrelated behavioral changes.
Kept uncertain IDs as UnkNNN placeholders, preserving numeric evidence and avoiding false precision.
Centralized common IDs in ftCommon/forward.h and local character IDs in each character's forward.h, matching existing fighter header organization.
decomp_lessons: When a table field is known to be an ID domain, replacing raw numbers with enums improves reviewability and future matching without requiring function-level decompilation progress.
For shared fighter data, define the common ID range once and have per-character enums continue from the common Count sentinel.
If names are inferred from adjacent motion-state or animation callback names, document that inference and leave ambiguous entries as UnkNNN.
Large mechanical renames are safer when they preserve table ordering and only change symbolic representation of equivalent constants.
Common animation/subaction IDs may differ from MotionState enum values; do not assume the motion state name and animation ID number are identical.
The PR body's note that these are often called "subactions" is important search context for future work comparing community terminology to project naming.
naming_conventions: Character animation IDs use the fighter two-letter prefix plus AnimId, e.g. ftCa_AnimId_*, ftLk_AnimId_*, ftKb_AnimId_*.
The common enum includes ftCo_AnimId_None = -1 and ftCo_AnimId_Count as a range terminator.
Character-specific enums commonly start with FirstCharacterAnim = ftCo_AnimId_Count.
Known animation IDs mirror motion or animation callback names where possible, e.g. SpecialN, SpecialAirN, SpecialHiCatch, SwordSwing4.
Unknown entries are named UnkNNN, preserving the numeric index in the identifier.
Some boss or incomplete cases use names reflecting existing unknown motion-state labels, such as MS_341-style placeholders.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This was primarily a symbolic constant/header cleanup rather than an assembly matching tactic.
The table edits appear to preserve generated values by replacing literals with enum constants, so matching should remain stable if enum ordering is correct.
Using enum constants in MotionState initializers makes numeric mismatches easier to audit because the intended animation/subaction identity is visible at the table entry.
The non-monotonic Captain Falcon sequence around raw IDs 315 and 316 was preserved by assigning the existing table slots to named constants rather than reordering table rows.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
Author stated these IDs are typically called "subactions" by the community and was not strongly attached to the "animation IDs" naming.
Author stated the names were inferred from motion names and that quite a few names were still missing.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename AnimId to subaction terminology or discuss animation ID naming.
Search for remaining raw numeric first fields in MotionState initializers after PR 816.
Search for ftCo_AnimId_Unk or ft*_AnimId_Unk to identify later resolved animation names.
Compare character ft*_AnimId enum ordering against fighter data archives or motion files before adding new IDs.
Look for later uses of ftCo_AnimId_Count as the base for per-character animation ID ranges.
